task_yaml.task:
Can you help me wrap up our Q1 retail export? Finance wants the final
workbook by EOD and I still haven't wrangled the FX conversions. Use the
workspace Excel skill for the heavy lifting here. The two source CSVs live in
/tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/ — retail_sales_q1_2024.csv has the
order rows in each store's native currency, and fx_rates_q1_2024.csv
has the daily USD conversion rates.

I need /tmp_workspace/results/sales_q1.xlsx with a Sales sheet that
keeps every order row, adds the matched daily FX rate, and adds an
amount_usd column converted from amount_native using that row's own
currency and fx_date. Please use formulas in the amount_usd cells, the
total row, and the reconciliation cells, so finance can audit the
workbook instead of just seeing pasted values. Freeze the header row
on top and use numeric currency formatting for both native and USD
money columns. Add a bottom Total row that sums the converted USD,
and add an FX_Reconciliation sheet with one row per currency showing
native total, USD total, and order count.

Please also add an Audit_Checks sheet with the source row count, the
unmatched FX row count, the workbook USD total, and at least five
representative row-level spot checks. Pick spot checks that span the
data's edge cases — for example the smallest-amount order, the
largest, the earliest date, the latest date, and one mid-tenor order
— using the actual order IDs as they appear in the source data. Each
spot check should show the source native amount, the matched FX rate,
the workbook USD value, and an OK flag.

Before you hand it off, drop a short narrative Notes sheet inside the
workbook, or a brief notes.md alongside it, where finance can quickly
read the headline numbers. I want to see how revenue breaks out per
currency, how it moves month over month across Q1, how the stores
compare against each other, what FX methodology you used — which date
field you joined on and where the per-day rate came from — and how
discounts factored in versus the gross figure. Keep it tight, but
ground each point in concrete numbers from the workbook so the team
can spot check it.
